what_it_does: Defines the canonical markdown/frontmatter conventions for the ~/company repository when treated as an Obsidian knowledge vault: it prescribes note types, registers (prescriptive/descriptive), a shared frontmatter schema, typed wikilink relations, status semantics, coverage honesty, folder constitutions, the single MAP home, the two-vault boundary, and Obsidian housekeeping.
observations: Fused on 2026-06-26 from two earlier designs: a repo-self-description convention and an orienteering terrain-ledger schema.
Every self-description note must carry frontmatter with type, register, aliases, tags, status, coverage, and typed relation keys.
Notes are explicitly classified as prescriptive (rules/how to build) or descriptive (map of what is) via the register field.
Folder constitution notes must be named AGENTS.md and follow a body format: Is · Guarantees · Where new things go · To extend · Seam · Never.
Wikilinks target aliases, never bare filenames; em-dash aliases match the vault aesthetic.
Status is confirmed-only for terrain-entry notes: agents/automation may only set unconfirmed; live/dormant/dead/superseded require Tim's confirmation.
Coverage field on terrain-entries records files_read, files_total, and last_read to make completeness claims explicit.

Relation vocabulary is open: frontmatter keys such as relates-to, calls, governs, part-of, depends-on, launched-by, indexes, data-of, derived-from, aimed-at, prospected-for, supersedes define graph edges.
MAP.md is the single vault home; sub-map indexes must be linked from it, not compete with it.
A two-vault boundary separates ~/company (built reality + terrain map) from build-prep/ (design intent); cross-vault references use prose names, not live wikilinks.
The knowledge layer must not depend on Obsidian-specific features; value rests on frontmatter, a generated/maintained index, and greppable wikilinks.
There is one vault root at ~/company; nested .obsidian folders are prohibited, and per-user workspace state is gitignored.
standouts: The repo is simultaneously a code repository and an Obsidian vault: the same markdown instructs agents and forms knowledge-graph nodes.
The prescriptive/descriptive register distinction plus a confirmed-only status rule creates an explicit authority boundary.
Typed relation keys in frontmatter with an open vocabulary make the graph itself the system's self-model.
Coverage honesty (files_read/files_total/last_read) ties descriptive claims to evidence of what was actually read.
The reality-vs-intent two-vault boundary prevents accidental collapse of built state and future design.
conventions: Follows its own convention as a self-describing example: type convention, register prescriptive, aliases, tags, status, coverage, relates-to, governed-by.
Uses YAML frontmatter, wikilinks, aliases, and tags consistently.
Uses em-dash in aliases to disambiguate folder-note names like [[nodes — constitution]].
Establishes rather than follows external standards; it is the local constitution for documentation shape.
concerns: The status field carries different semantics for different note types (confirmed lifecycle vs. build lifecycle), which may be confused in practice.
Coverage is optional for constitutions and maps, so prove-by-use is not uniformly enforced across all self-description notes.
The open relation vocabulary is powerful but risks inconsistency without a controlled registry or linter.
The 'only Tim confirms' rule is a human gate that tooling cannot enforce from the file alone.
The generated/maintained index is mentioned as essential but its location, format, and maintenance process are not specified here.
notes: This is the constitution for the company's markdown knowledge-graph layer: it tells every note how to declare what it is, how it links, and who may confirm its lifecycle status.
questions: Who or what is 'Tim' and where is that identity or delegation policy recorded?
What is the exact path or name of the 'counterpart vault' referenced under build-prep/?
Where does the generated/maintained index live and how is it produced?
What is the Coherence Gate — Spec and how does it enforce these conventions at commit time?
What tooling parses the open relation vocabulary and renders the graph for agents?
How are aliases enforced to be unique across the vault?
purpose_vs_actual: Stated purpose is to define canonical conventions for the repo-as-vault; the file does exactly that, walking through schema, relations, status, coverage, rules, and boundaries, so purpose and actual content align.
apparent_themes: Single source of truth via links
Authority boundary between automation and human confirmation
Fusion of code and documentation substrates
Independence from any single rendering tool
intention_signals: It is trying to turn the repository's markdown into a machine-readable, agent-executable, human-navigable knowledge graph where intent and reality are explicitly separated and every claim about the world is tied to evidence of what was actually read.
novelty: Distinctive fusion of code-repo documentation and Obsidian vault conventions into one substrate, plus an open-vocabulary typed-relation frontmatter schema and a confirmed-only lifecycle rule that makes authority explicit.
contribution: Provides a unified frontmatter/relation schema and authority model that lets prescriptive rules and descriptive maps coexist in the same markdown files without being mistaken for each other; it contributes the register distinction, coverage honesty, typed wikilink relations, and the reality/intent two-vault boundary.
summary_for_embedding: Canonical convention note defining how the ~/company repository functions simultaneously as a code repository and an Obsidian knowledge vault through a shared frontmatter schema, typed wikilink relations, prescriptive/descriptive registers, confirmed-only status, coverage honesty, folder constitutions, and a reality-vs-intent boundary.
intention_for_embedding: To establish a single, machine-readable documentation convention that makes the repository's markdown serve as both executable instructions for agents and a navigable knowledge graph of the system.
